annotations.label: Qubit

anchor.snippet: the qubit is the fundamental unit of quantum information

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of qubit or quantum information; only generic complex vector-space infrastructure exists.

annotations.label: Superposition of states

anchor.snippet: Such a combination is known as a superposition

annotations.note: Linear combinations of vectors are formalized generally, but the quantum notion of superposition is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Qubit state and probability amplitudes

anchor.snippet: any qubit state takes the form

annotations.note: No qubit state or probability-amplitude representation is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Born rule for qubit measurement

anchor.snippet: The Born rule describes the norm-squared correspondence between amplitudes and probabilities

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize quantum measurement or the Born rule.

annotations.label: Normalization of amplitudes

anchor.snippet: Any valid qubit state has coefficients

annotations.note: Unit-norm vectors in inner-product spaces exist generally, but the qubit normalization condition |α|²+|β|²=1 is not a stated result in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Measuring an equal-superposition qubit

anchor.snippet: As an example, measuring the qubit

annotations.note: Quantum measurement outcomes and their probabilities are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Plus and minus states

anchor.snippet: Two particularly important superposition states are the plus state

annotations.note: The |+⟩/|−⟩ qubit basis states are not defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hadamard gate

anchor.snippet: they behave differently under operations such as the Hadamard gate

annotations.note: Mathlib's 'Hadamard' refers to the three-lines theorem and the matrix Hadamard (entrywise) product, not the quantum Hadamard gate.

annotations.label: Each qubit doubles the state-space dimension

anchor.snippet: Each additional qubit doubles the dimension of the state space

annotations.note: Mathlib proves dim(M ⊗ N) = dim M · dim N, which generalizes dimension-doubling when tensoring with a 2-dimensional space, but says nothing about qubits.

annotations.label: Two-qubit tensor product state

anchor.snippet: represents a two-qubit state, a tensor product of the qubit

annotations.note: The tensor product of modules/vector spaces is fully formalized, generalizing the construction of multi-qubit states, though qubits themselves are absent.

annotations.label: Exponential dimension of n-qubit space

anchor.snippet: the vector space for an n -qubit system is 2 n -dimensional

annotations.note: The dimension of a tensor product being the product of dimensions yields 2^n as a special case, but the n-qubit statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: NOT gate

anchor.snippet: One important gate for both classical and quantum computation is the NOT gate

annotations.note: The Pauli-X / NOT gate is not defined; only generic unitary matrices (Matrix.unitaryGroup) exist.

anchors.snippet: One important gate for both classical and quantum computation is the NOT gate

annotations.label: Controlled NOT (CNOT) gate

anchor.snippet: The controlled NOT (CNOT) gate can then be represented using the following matrix

annotations.note: The CNOT gate is not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The controlled NOT (CNOT) gate can then be represented using the following matrix

annotations.label: Deferred measurement

anchor.snippet: any measurement can be deferred to the end of quantum computation

annotations.note: The deferred-measurement principle of quantum circuits is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum parallelism

anchor.snippet: Quantum parallelism is the heuristic that quantum computers can be thought of as evaluating a function for multiple input values simultaneously

annotations.note: Quantum parallelism is not a concept present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Universal gate set

anchor.snippet: A choice of gate family that enables this construction is known as a universal gate set

annotations.note: Quantum gates and universal gate sets are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solovay–Kitaev theorem

anchor.snippet: it can be replaced with a finite gate set by appealing to the Solovay-Kitaev theorem

annotations.note: The Solovay–Kitaev theorem is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Measurement-based quantum computer

anchor.snippet: A measurement-based quantum computer decomposes computation into a sequence of Bell state measurements

annotations.note: Measurement-based quantum computing and Bell-state measurements are not formalized.

annotations.label: Adiabatic quantum computer

anchor.snippet: based on quantum annealing , decomposes computation into a slow continuous transformation

annotations.note: Adiabatic quantum computing is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neuromorphic quantum computing

anchor.snippet: Neuromorphic quantum computing (abbreviated 'n.quantum computing') is an unconventional process of computing

annotations.note: Neuromorphic quantum computing has no representation in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Topological quantum computer

anchor.snippet: A topological quantum computer decomposes computation into the braiding of anyons

annotations.note: Anyons and topological quantum computation are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum Turing machine

anchor.snippet: A quantum Turing machine is the quantum analog of a Turing machine

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes classical Turing machines (Mathlib.Computability.TuringMachine) but not the quantum analog.

annotations.label: Equivalence of quantum computation models

anchor.snippet: have been shown to be equivalent to the quantum Turing machine

annotations.note: Equivalence of quantum models of computation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Threshold theorem

anchor.snippet: The threshold theorem shows how increasing the number of qubits can mitigate errors

annotations.note: The quantum fault-tolerance threshold theorem is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum teleportation

anchor.snippet: Quantum teleportation is a protocol by which Alice can transmit the quantum state of a qubit to Bob

annotations.note: Quantum teleportation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Superdense coding

anchor.snippet: Superdense coding is the complementary protocol

annotations.note: Superdense coding is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Holevo's theorem

anchor.snippet: This appears to violate Holevo's theorem

annotations.note: Holevo's theorem and quantum information bounds are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Super-polynomial speedup of Shor's algorithm

anchor.snippet: include Shor's algorithm for factoring

annotations.note: Shor's algorithm and its complexity are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: BQP-complete problems

anchor.snippet: have quantum algorithms appearing to give super-polynomial speedups and are BQP -complete

annotations.note: The complexity class BQP and BQP-completeness are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polynomial speedup of Grover's algorithm

anchor.snippet: give polynomial speedups over corresponding classical algorithms

annotations.note: Grover's algorithm and its speedup are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unstructured search speedup

anchor.snippet: The most well-known example of a problem that allows for a polynomial quantum speedup is unstructured search

annotations.note: Quantum unstructured-search speedup is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Optimality of Grover's algorithm

anchor.snippet: the advantage is not only provable but also optimal

annotations.note: The optimality (BBBV lower bound) of Grover's algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conditions for applying Grover's algorithm

anchor.snippet: Problems that can be efficiently addressed with Grover's algorithm have the following properties

annotations.note: Conditions for applying Grover's algorithm are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Shor's algorithm factors integers exponentially faster

anchor.snippet: a quantum computer could solve this problem exponentially faster using Shor's algorithm to factor the integer

annotations.note: Shor's integer-factoring speedup is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Grover speedup on symmetric ciphers

anchor.snippet: applying Grover's algorithm to break a symmetric (secret-key) algorithm by brute force requires time equal to roughly

annotations.note: The application of Grover's algorithm to symmetric-key brute force is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum annealing

anchor.snippet: Quantum annealing uses the adiabatic theorem to perform calculations

annotations.note: Quantum annealing is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adiabatic theorem

anchor.snippet: The adiabatic theorem states that if the evolution is slow enough, the system will stay in its ground state

annotations.note: The quantum adiabatic theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classical problems are quantum-solvable

anchor.snippet: Any computational problem solvable by a classical computer is also solvable by a quantum computer

annotations.note: Mathlib has classical computability but no quantum model, so this classical-to-quantum simulation claim is not formalized.

annotations.label: Quantum problems are classically solvable

anchor.snippet: any problem solvable by a quantum computer is also solvable by a classical computer

annotations.note: No quantum computation model exists in Mathlib, so this quantum-to-classical simulation claim is not formalized.

annotations.label: BQP complexity class

anchor.snippet: The class of problems that can be efficiently solved by a quantum computer with bounded error is called BQP

annotations.note: The complexity class BQP is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P ⊆ BQP ⊆ PSPACE

anchor.snippet: all problems that can be efficiently solved by a deterministic classical computer can also be efficiently solved by a quantum computer

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the complexity classes P, BQP, or PSPACE or their inclusions.

annotations.label: Quantum supremacy

anchor.snippet: coined the term quantum supremacy to describe the engineering feat

annotations.note: Quantum supremacy is not a formalized concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Halting problem

anchor.snippet: quantum computers cannot solve undecidable problems like the halting problem

annotations.note: Mathlib's PartrecCode develops Kleene's recursion/fixed-point theorem and the framework underlying undecidability of the halting problem, though a named 'halting problem is undecidable' theorem is not stated.

annotations.label: Church–Turing thesis not disproved by quantum computers

anchor.snippet: the existence of quantum computers does not disprove the Church–Turing thesis

annotations.note: The Church–Turing thesis is an informal claim and is not formalized as a theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integer factorization

anchor.snippet: it is known that quantum computers can efficiently factor integers

annotations.note: Mathlib has the mathematical notion of prime factorization (Nat.factorization, UniqueFactorizationMonoid) but no notion of computational complexity of factoring.

annotations.label: Discrete logarithm problem

anchor.snippet: the related quantum algorithms for computing discrete logarithms

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the discrete logarithm problem as a computational problem.

annotations.label: Hidden subgroup problem

anchor.snippet: solving the hidden subgroup problem for abelian finite groups

annotations.note: The hidden subgroup problem is not formalized in Mathlib, though abelian finite groups and their characters are.

annotations.label: Quantum Fourier transform

anchor.snippet: These algorithms depend on the primitive of the quantum Fourier transform

annotations.note: Mathlib has the classical discrete Fourier transform on finite abelian groups but not the unitary quantum Fourier transform on qubit registers.

annotations.label: Ising model

anchor.snippet: Quantum annealing can solve Ising models and the (computationally equivalent) QUBO problem

annotations.note: The Ising model and QUBO problem are not formalized in Mathlib.
